The rain didn't wash away the dirt; it just smeared the grime further across the pavement. Detective Elias Vance stood under the awning of the 24-hour bodega, clutching a coffee cup that felt increasingly like a weapon. His mind was racing, syncing with the erratic pulse of the woman standing ten feet away. 

That wasn't normal. She hadn't walked past him yet; she had been speaking to him for three minutes straight, though her lips were never moving. Every few seconds, his brain would catch a fragment of her inner monologue: *Stop breathing*, *Don't turn around*, *He can hear everything*. It was a cruel game of telephone played on nerves.

Elias took a sharp breath, counting the rhythm of the thunder. One minute was twenty-two seconds; another minute was eighteen. Another ten seconds, and he would lose the thread.

Suddenly, the connection snapped tight enough to choke him. He could feel the sudden spike of adrenaline in her chest. *He knows*, her voice echoed in his skull, louder than the street noise. *They're coming.*

Elias turned sharply, the motion sending a wave of static through his ears. He needed the name, the signature detail to lock her onto the file. He waited for the silence, for the ten seconds of void between impulses. The clock ticked down silently in his mind. Nine seconds left. Eight. Seven. Six. Five. Four. Three.

She froze. Her eyes darted to the shadows behind the car doors. He reached for his notebook, the leather scratching softly against his palm.

Two.

One.

The link severed completely. A ringing sensation filled his head, like a gavel striking bone. Elias looked at the woman, then back toward the alleyway where two figures were emerging from the darkness. He knew exactly who they were, but without the auditory tether, he couldn't confirm their purpose.

The detective took a deep, shuddering breath. Ten seconds was a lifetime of memory compressed into a heartbeat, and he had lost it all again. He watched the shadowed men approach, waiting for the next glimpse of consciousness to save his night—and his life—from slipping through the cracks of time.<|im_end|>

The rain didn't wash away the dirt; it just smeared the grime further across the pavement. Detective Elias Vance stood under the awning of the 24-hour bodega, clutching a coffee cup that felt increasingly like a weapon. His mind was racing, syncing with the erratic pulse of the woman standing ten feet away. 

That wasn't normal. She hadn't walked past him yet; she had been speaking to him for three minutes straight, though her lips were never moving. Every few seconds, his brain would catch a fragment of her inner monologue: *Stop breathing*, *Don't turn around*, *He can hear everything*. It was a cruel game of telephone played on nerves.

Elias took a sharp breath, counting the rhythm of the thunder. One minute was twenty-two seconds; another minute was eighteen. Another ten seconds, and he would lose the thread.

Suddenly, the connection snapped tight enough to choke him. He could feel the sudden spike of adrenaline in her chest. *He knows*, her voice echoed in his skull, louder than the street noise. *They're coming.*

Elias turned sharply, the motion sending a wave of static through his ears. He needed the name, the signature detail to lock her onto the file. He waited for the silence, for the ten seconds of void between impulses. The clock ticked down silently in his mind. Nine seconds left. Eight. Seven. Six. Five. Four. Three.

She froze. Her eyes darted to the shadows behind the car doors. He reached for his notebook, the leather scratching softly against his palm.

Two.

One.

The link severed completely. A ringing sensation filled his head, like a gavel striking bone. Elias looked at the woman, then back toward the alleyway where two figures were emerging from the darkness. He knew exactly who they were, but without the auditory tether, he couldn't confirm their purpose.

The detective took a deep, shuddering breath. Ten seconds was a lifetime of memory compressed into a heartbeat, and he had lost it all again. He watched the shadowed men approach, waiting for the next glimpse of consciousness to save his night—and his life—from slipping through the cracks of time.<|im_end|>
